Learn Sanskrit to Read Ancient Texts : Lesson 1 - Warming Up : Giving Complements & Calling People

# Learn Sanskrit to Read Ancient Texts : Lesson 1 - Warming Up : Giving Complements & Calling People

### The text that I have written to teach Sanskrit to people is publicly uploaded here as a series of Substack articles.

[](https://substack.com/@ramaseshanc)

![Rama Seshan Chandrasekaran's avatar](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!ZHOm!,w_36,h_36,c_fill,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F11e50d60-b811-4831-8bbc-4d320762e60b_96x96.png)

[Rama Seshan Chandrasekaran](https://substack.com/@ramaseshanc) and [CSHC](https://substack.com/@cshc)

Mar 18, 2026

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

10

Share

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!Qu5W!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F57fb9c52-a9de-4984-935b-b528269da505_1416x857.jpeg)

![undefined](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!Qu5W!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F57fb9c52-a9de-4984-935b-b528269da505_1416x857.jpeg "undefined")

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

This is a series of articles that helps an Indian who is already familiar with the Devanāgari script to learn Sanskrit by a combination of immersion and a little grammar as and when required. This is based on an e-book that I have compiled to teach Sanskrit in my circle. This series of articles is organised fairly intuitively but there are some points that are worth putting forth right at the beginning itself. This is based on an extensive set of reading and translation exercises; both in-text and as end-of-chapter exercises. Since Sanskrit vocabulary is very vast, I understand that you cannot master most of them within the duration of this small series of articles. Vocabulary building will hence be a gradual process. To facilitate this, any Sanskrit word in this book (except proper names) is glossed with meaning when it occurs for the first time.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

# Stages of the Sanskrit language

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

The Sanskrit language historically is divided broadly into the following stages:

1.  Vedic Sanskrit

2.  Post Vedic Sanskrit

3.  Epic Sanskrit

4.  Classical Sanskrit

5.  Pali and the dramatic Prakrits

6.  Buddhist Hybrid Sanskrit

7.  Modern Sanskrit

The language that we will be focusing on in this course for the most part will be Classical Sanskrit whose grammar was described and hence standardised by the grammarian **Pāṇini** around the 6th to 5th centuries BCE. While the term Classical Sanskrit technically is reserved only for the language codified by Pāṇini, it is mutually intelligible with Post Vedic Sanskrit and Epic Sanskrit. But, a brief introduction to the various stages of Sanskrit is given in this section.

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!ZPYq!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F64c9e5ce-e422-42c2-bc95-ff416ff9803b_2048x1163.png)

![](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!ZPYq!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F64c9e5ce-e422-42c2-bc95-ff416ff9803b_2048x1163.png "Screenshot 2025-09-18 at 12.57.39.png")

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

The term Vedic Sanskrit refers to the oldest stage of the Sanskrit language as attested in the earliest of Vedic literature; especially the Ṛg-veda saṃhitā and the saṃhitās of the other two older vedas (and to some extent, the brāhmaṇas as well) . This Vedic Sanskrit is not mutually intelligible with the later Classical Sanskrit and requires some extra training as much as a normal English speaker would require extra training to comprehend the works of Chaucer or Shakespeare. This language is highly poetic as well in addition to being archaic in nature. We will study Vedic Sanskrit in some detail, only in the final part of this course. The language simplifies to some extent as one approaches the Āraṇyakas, which are later compositions. By the time of the Upaniṣads, the language entered what is known as the post-Vedic stage which is highly mutually intelligible with Classical Sanskrit.

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!s8IG!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2Ff140ae1a-4810-4ca1-b00f-c8ad8ae18171_2048x1157.png)

![](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!s8IG!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2Ff140ae1a-4810-4ca1-b00f-c8ad8ae18171_2048x1157.png "Screenshot 2025-09-18 at 12.58.05.png")

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

The term Post Vedic Sanskrit refers to the stage of the language that starts with the Upaniṣads and forms as a bridge to Classical Sanskrit. Important works in this stage are the vedāṅgas, early śāstras, and the sūtra type literature. The language is intelligible with the Classical stage and many features of the classical language are already seen to be established. Some of the major differences between Vedic and Post-Vedic / Classical Sanskrit are:

1.  **Loss of vocabulary** : Some of the vocabulary used in the Vedic
    language become archaic and are lost or hardly used in the classical
    language or they change their meaning.

2.  **Loss of tenses and moods** : About half of the tenses and moods
    that occur for Vedic Sanskrit verbs are lost in Classical Sanskrit.

3.  **Loss of pitch accent** : Vedic Sanskrit had a pitch accent system
    wherein there were three pitches with which a vowel could be uttered
    and the meanings of words too sometimes changed depending on what
    vowel of the word where the main accent fell. This accentuation is
    completely lost in Classical Sanskrit.

4.  **Grammatical changes** : Some grammatical endings change when we go
    from Vedic to Classical Sanskrit and variant endings for the same
    scenario get standardised and streamlined into one ending.

5.  **Past tenses** : The three types of past tenses for verbs in Vedic
    Sanskrit that had clean and clear cut distinctions in meaning became
    synonymous in Classical Sanskrit.

6.  **Long Compounds and Sandhi** : The formation of compound words and
    sandhi changes between words that were spontaneous in the Vedic
    language became standardised and one begins to see the same sandhi
    rules between all words and long compounds that would never be used
    much in the Vedic language.

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!XEAO!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F798df889-e5e7-49a9-91de-27f9c9a096ad_2048x1133.png)

![](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!XEAO!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F798df889-e5e7-49a9-91de-27f9c9a096ad_2048x1133.png "Screenshot 2025-09-18 at 12.58.29.png")

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

An important stage in the history of Sanskrit is the epic stage where the two epics Rāmāyaṇa and Mahābhārata were composed. Although a part of the generic post-Vedic stage and mutually intelligible with the post Vedic and Classical Sanskrit, it deserves a special mention that contains its own mixtures of archaic and contemporary styles and also takes some poetic liberties. The language of the epics deviates from Pāṇinian rules not just because these are pre-Pāṇinian but these are genuine innovations with variant forms being present sometimes within the same stanza. Some of these innovations are due to metrical exigencies while others are simply innovations that just did not make its way to be attested in the grammar of the classical language. Also sometimes, these aberrations are Prakritisations or influences from the vernacular Prakrits.

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!PS5B!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F0d430ac0-77e9-42a1-b979-5e3fd49f76cc_2048x1158.png)

![](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!PS5B!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F0d430ac0-77e9-42a1-b979-5e3fd49f76cc_2048x1158.png "Screenshot 2025-09-18 at 12.59.09.png")

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

The standard form of the Sanskrit language is that of the classical stage whose grammar was extensively described by **Pāṇini**. This froze the language for the most part and Pāṇini’s description then evolved to become a prescription for the times after him. The process of introducing new words and grammatical deviations continued for a few more centuries after Pāṇini until they were regularised and incorporated by the later grammarian **Vararuci** (who wrote the **Vārtika**s) and finally by **Patañjali** (who wrote the **Vyākaraṇamahābhāṣya**). From then on, any violation of the rules as laid down by Patañjali was regarded as a grammatical error and the Sanskrit language essentially remained the same from then on to this day, despite a proliferation of much more compositions (ranging from **kāvya**s, **purāṇa**s, some **śāstra**s, philosophical, scientific, and mathematical works) . This standard became a benchmark as to what was correct Sanskrit (grammatical in the Pāṇinian sense) and what is not.

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!JL3x!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F047854f7-ef57-49a0-b632-9bc74482a34a_2048x1171.png)

![](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!JL3x!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F047854f7-ef57-49a0-b632-9bc74482a34a_2048x1171.png "Screenshot 2025-09-18 at 12.59.35.png")

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

While the classical language of Sanskrit remained unchanged as a literary language for millenia, the spoken vernaculars did indeed continue the processes of innovation, and simplification which are collectively called as Prakrits. There were lots of Prakrits attested in various regions with which Sanskrit co-existed in a diglossic manner. Some of these Prakrits came to be attested in Classical Sanskrit dramas where the brahmin men and kings speak Sanskrit and the women and the common men speak Prakrits (although the dramatic authors provide a translation into Classical Sanskrit of these Prakritic dialogues as well). Prakrits also made their way into the literatures of early Buddhism and Jainism. One such famous Prakrit is the Pali language which is the language in which the earliest Buddhists canon of texts (**tripiṭaka**s) have come down to us which is the sacred canon of the **Theravāda** (Sanskrit : **Sthaviravāda**) Buddhism that is practised in Śrī Laṅkā, Thailand, Burma, Cambodia, and the South-East Asia. Although the Buddha himself spoke an Eastern variety of Prakrit from Bihar, the Pali language is based on a Western Prakrit with influences from other dialects as well as the Pali canon was written down only three centuries after the times of the Buddha.

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!L7T0!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F8d0c5a34-64e7-4fb6-a6ab-0c6b382a2963_2048x1162.png)

![](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!L7T0!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F8d0c5a34-64e7-4fb6-a6ab-0c6b382a2963_2048x1162.png "Screenshot 2025-09-18 at 12.59.59.png")

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

Around the first century before Christ, a new group of Buddhist texts that also claimed to be teachings of the Buddha, began to emerge and would become a part of the sacred canon of what is later the **Mahāyāna** Buddhism that would soon become the dominant branch of Buddhism practised in North India (till the fall of Buddhism in India) and also spread to East Asia (China, Tibet, and Japan). The language of these canon of sacred texts is traditionally considered to be Sanskrit but is actually Prakrit in terms of grammar and syntax while only phonologically Sanskritizing the pronunciation. This language is known as **Buddhist Hybrid Sanskrit** in academia. However, as time went by and philosophical debates became more important and frequent, even the Buddhists and Jains in India adopted Classical Sanskrit for their compositions.

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!bOx5!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2Fb2c64c4f-e8ff-4d5a-b283-c7a8e47ef5cb_2048x1156.png)

![](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!bOx5!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2Fb2c64c4f-e8ff-4d5a-b283-c7a8e47ef5cb_2048x1156.png "Screenshot 2025-09-18 at 13.00.19.png")

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

In the modern times, a much more simplified form of Sanskrit without sandhi and without many of the tense forms of Classical Sanskrit began to emerge in the efforts to resurrect Sanskrit as a modern language and is now taught by organisations like Samskrita Bharati. This language uses a much more restricted set of grammatical forms and words of the classical language and also deviates in syntax from classical usage (adopting a more vernacular style of syntax found in modern Indo-Aryan languages). While technically called Sanskrit, the gap between this Modern simplified Sanskrit and Classical Sanskrit is almost as big as the gap between Classical Sanskrit and Vedic Sanskrit.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

# Formulaic Adjectives & Epithets

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

In this first lesson, let us start with something light. We will get introduced to an important concept in Sanskrit poetry. Remember that most of Sanskrit literature is poetry and hence this point is important. Often, the poet is in a situation where he has to satisfy certain metrical constraints. So, if adjectives are liberally added to simply satisfy certain metrical requirements even though they may not be directly relevant to the situation. Most of them turn out to be epithets that complement the hero or bring down the villain. These are called **formulaic adjectives** ; adjectives that are employed in identical metrical constraints. We will learn some frequently occurring ones and general techniques used to form a broad class of them so that you can easily recognise them. As one of my friends to whom I taught Sanskrit said, Sanskrit texts suffer from severe ***adjectivitis*** !! There are lots of adjectives for a given noun. One does not simply say “*Rama released an arrow*” but says something like “*The foremost ornament of the Raghu dynasty, the glorious Rama, having readied his bow, released that sharp, golden-feathered, poison-smeared arrow, swift as Garuḍa’s flight, spreading with the speed equal to the wind, restrained (only) by the quiver of his arrow-case*”. In this section, we will take it light and explore some of these popular adjectives and epithets and how they are created.

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!9820!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F26f948d5-55cb-489d-a7a3-a4ff1fc4570f_2048x893.png)

![](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!9820!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F26f948d5-55cb-489d-a7a3-a4ff1fc4570f_2048x893.png "Screenshot 2025-09-18 at 13.17.57.png")

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

**पुङ्गवः - bull** : This word is made of two parts - **पुं** (male) + **गवः** (cow) which gives bull. This word meaning literally bull generally denotes strength - both physical and mental. Often it comes as a compound like आर्यपुङ्गवः *bull among the āryas*, राजपुङ्गवः *bull among the king*s, मुनिपुङ्गवः *bull among the munis*, etc.

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!ACrq!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F7703d2b6-8954-464f-868d-891e86e319df_2048x1095.png)

![](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!ACrq!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F7703d2b6-8954-464f-868d-891e86e319df_2048x1095.png "Screenshot 2025-09-18 at 13.18.24.png")

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

**ज्ञः - knower** : This word literally means knower and denotes being knowledgeable. It too comes as a part of a compound word that gives more context. For example, धर्मज्ञः *one who is knowledgeable in dharma*, सर्वज्ञः *all-knowing*, वेदज्ञः *one who is knowledgeable in the vedas*, त्रिकालज्ञः *one who knows all three times*, तत्वज्ञः *knowledgeable in tattvas*. Another famous epithet of this type is कृतज्ञः which literally means *one who knows what has been done*. But कृतज्ञः actually means “*grateful*” because a person with gratitude remembers what good others have done for him.

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!O6rS!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2Fa5f0fbde-427e-42d0-b0c1-c7033aeb3420_2048x921.png)

![](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!O6rS!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2Fa5f0fbde-427e-42d0-b0c1-c7033aeb3420_2048x921.png "Screenshot 2025-09-18 at 13.18.43.png")

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

**कोविदः - expert** : This means expert and also comes with suffixes that usually go with ज्ञः. For example, in the **Nalopākhyāna**, the king Nala is described as अश्वकोविदः which means *expert in horse (riding)*.

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!CMu0!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2Ff7401fff-edd6-4336-9733-b66feaae5acb_2048x1029.png)

![](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!CMu0!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2Ff7401fff-edd6-4336-9733-b66feaae5acb_2048x1029.png "Screenshot 2025-09-18 at 13.19.06.png")

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

**युक्तः / उपेतः / सम्पन्नः - endowed / equipped / supplied**: This word युक्तः literally means *joined or yoked with* but is used to mean commonly *endowed with* or *equipped with* which it shares with उपेतः and सम्पन्नः . But the question to answer is equipped “with” what? So, the thing the person is equipped with comes as the first part of a compound. For example, consider चारित्रयुक्तः *equipped with (good) conduct*, सर्वगुणोपेतः/सर्वगुणसम्पन्नः *equipped with all (good) qualities*, and हितयुक्तः *endowed with good deeds*. But many times, this also comes as an important adjective in context rather than as a decorative epithet like राज्य-भार-युक्तः *supplied with the burden of kingship*. Next comes the class of epithets that complement body parts.

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!8acf!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F9bbb1b9a-1a02-4cd8-8b35-91fac61ba3a1_2048x1121.png)

![](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!8acf!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F9bbb1b9a-1a02-4cd8-8b35-91fac61ba3a1_2048x1121.png "Screenshot 2025-09-18 at 13.19.32.png")

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

Some important epithets complementing the body and body parts for males are प्रियदर्शनः *one who has good looks*, महाबाहुः *big armed*, बाहुबली *having strong shoulders*, महाहनुः *chubby cheeked*, कम्बुग्रीवः *having neck like a conch*, विपुलांसः *broad-shouldered*, विशालाक्षः *expansive-eyed* (metaphorically extends to mean *broad sighted*), सम-विभक्ताङ्गः *having well-proportioned-limbs*, स्निग्ध-वर्णः *shiny-complexioned*, आ-जानु-बाहुः *one whose hands touch the knees*, महोरस्कः *big-chested*, etc. Often just a combination of the prefix सु *good* and a body part may be used like सुशिरः *good-head* and सुललाटः *good-forehead*.

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!-if5!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2Fbce38823-9c03-4683-a370-a1edc127e3df_1782x996.png)

![](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!-if5!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2Fbce38823-9c03-4683-a370-a1edc127e3df_1782x996.png "Screenshot 2026-02-08 at 08.28.53.png")

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

Some epithets for complementing body parts of females are सुमध्यमा *good-waisted*, समलङ्कृता *perfectly decorated*, अप्रतिमा *incomparable,* सर्वाभरणभूषिता *decorated by all ornaments*, आयतलोचना *broad-eyes*, रूपवती *beautiful (form-endowed)*, चित्तप्रमाथिनी *stirrer of \[other people’s\] minds*, सौदामिनी *lighting like shiny*, अनवद्याङ्गी *one with a faultless limbs*, मृद्वी *soft*, and चारुसर्वाङ्गी *one whose every limb is good*.

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!L7Gn!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F7a430ffe-75ab-4392-8973-2ea93c5b4121_1776x1004.png)

![](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!L7Gn!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F7a430ffe-75ab-4392-8973-2ea93c5b4121_1776x1004.png "Screenshot 2026-02-08 at 08.44.44.png")

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

There are also gender-neuter complementary adjectives, popular among which are given above.

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!XRxt!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F13c1225c-d0f0-4f9b-89b8-6c51da27be8e_2048x1088.png)

![](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!XRxt!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F13c1225c-d0f0-4f9b-89b8-6c51da27be8e_2048x1088.png "Screenshot 2025-09-18 at 13.19.53.png")

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

**सदृशः** like, **समः** equal to : These are standard suffixes for similes. The thing that is being likened to and equated to precedes the suffix. Examples would be कालाग्नि-सदृशः *like the all-consuming fire*, प्रजापतिसमः *like Prajāpati*, पृथिवीसमः \[patient\] *like the earth*, सर्वसमः *equal to everyone*, and so on.

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!giPS!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F19793af1-7f0e-4156-93f0-74a67feeb5c2_2048x1100.png)

![](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!giPS!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F19793af1-7f0e-4156-93f0-74a67feeb5c2_2048x1100.png "Screenshot 2025-09-18 at 13.20.18.png")

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

**मान्/वान्** (m) **वती/मती** (f) - **possessing** : These two are exclusively suffixes. Popular examples are quoted here only for the male form वीर्यवान् *possessing courage*, द्युतिमान् *possessing lustre*, धीमान् *possessing intelligence*, शक्तिमान् *possessing power*, भगवान् *possessing fortune (bhaga)*, श्रीमान् *possessing wealth*, प्रतापवान् *possessing energy*, नीतिमान् *possessing ethics*, स्मृतिमान् *possessing huge memory power*, हिमवान् *possessing the strength of the Himalayan mountain*s, and so on.

As an exercise, complement the men or women in your family by making up as many epithets from this template as possible ! Also, as another exercise, most of these epithets are found in the first chapter of the Rāmāyaṇa. So, even if you do not understand what is happening, go to this link by clicking [here](https://www.valmiki.iitk.ac.in/content?language=dv&field_kanda_tid=1&field_sarga_value=1&field_sloka_value=1) and find these epithets in the first chapter.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

# Calling People

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

In this section, we will see how to call people. Normally, in English, you do not change the form of the name of the person or object to call him or it. But some languages do. Even Hindi does - for instance :

> When talking about your son : **बेटा**  
> When calling your son : हे **बेटे**

Did you see the change in the ending there? Something similar happens in Latin too.

> When talking about Brutus : ***Brutus***  
> When calling Brutus : *Et tu, **Brute*** !! (You too, Brutus!)  
> (last words of Julius after he is stabbed by Brutus!)

Did you see how *Brutus* changed to *Brute* while he is being called? This happens a lot in Sanskrit and becomes familiar easily if you have heard Sanskrit shlokas yourself. The kind of changes are summarised in this table below.

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!lBZO!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2Fd367f288-ce2d-4f44-bf39-7cd8c88e70db_1566x984.png)

![](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!lBZO!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2Fd367f288-ce2d-4f44-bf39-7cd8c88e70db_1566x984.png "Screenshot 2026-02-08 at 08.13.22.png")

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

Learn the following patterns of calling. The change depends on the ending of the noun and on the gender of the person as well. Based on this, as an exercise, try who is being called in these compositions. That is, the words in bold from various popular compositions are people being called. Try decoding their form of mention (left entry on the table) from their address form.

> नमस्ते **सदावत्सले** **मातृभूमे**  
> ॐ **अम्बे** **गौरि**  
> **प्रभो** **गणपते**  
> **हरे कृष्ण हरे कृष्ण कृष्ण कृष्ण हरे हरे । हरे राम हरे राम राम राम हरे हरे**   
> नमस्ते ऽस्तु **महामाये** **श्रीपीठे** **सुरपूजिते** । **शङ्कचक्रगदाहस्ते**
> **महालक्ष्मि** नमो ऽस्तु ते

Now as a reverse practice, given below are mention forms of people or epithets. Figure out how will you directly address them.

> **वृद्धिः** → हे \_\_\_?  
> **मानसा** → हे \_\_\_?  
> **श्रीमान्** → हे \_\_\_?  
> **महाराजा** → हे \_\_\_?  
> **जगज्जननी** → हे \_\_\_?  
> **पुत्री** → हे \_\_\_?  
> **पुत्रः** → हे \_\_\_?  
> **आर्याम्बा** → हे \_\_\_?  
> **पुरुषव्याघ्रः** → हे \_\_\_?  
> **शक्तिः** → हे \_\_\_?

Note that in Sanskrit, the particle हे is emphatic and optional and hence do not rely on it to figure out who is being directly called. Rely only on the forms of endings.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

We have warmed up into the language by getting used to facts about the language, some classical pleasantries, and figuring out how to call people. Next time, we will jump into the pool !!

To read the next article in the series, click below this.

[](https://ramaseshanchandrasekaran.substack.com/p/learn-sanskrit-to-read-ancient-texts-96f)
